text: So, I want you to imagine a forest, but I don't want you to picture a nice, bright hiking trail.
I want you to picture a nightmare.
Right, like a really dark, scary place.
Exactly.
Think of these thick, ancient woods.
The ground is totally uneven.
There are hidden holes, sharp rocks, and the trees are so thick that they block out the sun.
It is dark.
It is confusing.
It is the kind of place where you can lose your way in just a few minutes.
Yeah, it is.
Now, imagine trying to fight a war in there.
You are wearing heavy armor, you are holding a sword or a spear, and every step is dangerous.
This is not a normal battlefield.
It is a death trap.
And our source material today, which is a Diffie Samuel chapter 18, tells us something really scary about this place.
It says the forest itself was a killer.
It says the wood devoured more people that day than the sword.
That is such a wild line.
It really is.
The word devoured means to eat hungrily.
So the forest ate the soldiers.
It sounds like a horror movie.
But this is the setting for one of the most intense stories in the Bible.
Today, we are doing a deep dive into 2 Samuel chapter 18.
And we are going to stay right here in this specific chapter.
Yes.
Our mission is to look at this text and see exactly how this story is told.
We are going to use the ideas of a scholar named Robert Alter.
He focuses on the art of biblical narrative.
Right.
The way the story is built.
Because this is not just a list of facts.
It is a master class in storytelling.
It is.
It is about a father's heart breaking and we get to watch it happen step by step.
So let's set the stage.
It is a civil war, but it is the worst kind of civil war because it is a family fighting each other.
It is King David fighting his own son Absalom.
Absalom has rebelled and wants to take the throne.
And before they even start fighting, we need to look at how David sets up his army because, well, it is a little strange.
We are right at the start of chapter 18.
David divides his army into three groups.
He puts three commanders in charge.
Joab.
His brother Abishai and a man named Ittai.
Okay, let's stop there for a second.
We know Joab.
Joab is the tough general.
He gets the job done no matter what.
He is very practical, very ruthless.
And we know Abishai.
But Ittai.
Who is Ittai?
The text calls him Ittai the Gittite.
That simply means he is from a city called Gath.
So he is a Philistine.
He is a foreigner.
Wait, so one of David's top three generals is not even from his own country.
Exactly.
He is basically a hired soldier.
A mercenary who promised to be loyal to David.
Why do you think the story tells us that right now?
Well, think about Robert Alter's ideas on how stories show us things without explaining them directly.
By mentioning this foreigner, the story creates a big contrast.
Oh, I see.
A contrast with his son.
Right.
You have this outsider who is perfectly loyal to the king.
And then you have the king's own son, Absalom, who is trying to kill him.
It shows us how broken the family is.
That is a really sad detail when you think about it.
So the army is ready to go.
And David stands up and tells his troops, I'm going out to fight with you.
He wants to be a leader.
But the troops, and there are thousands of them, they tell him no.
You cannot go with them.
Completely forbid him.
They say, if we run away, the enemy will not care.
If half of us die, the enemy will not care.
But you, you are worth 10,000 of us.
That is a very famous line.
You are worth 10,000.
At first I thought, wow, they really love him.
What a nice compliment.
But then I thought about it more.
Is it just a compliment?
Or is it just a compliment?
Or are they saying, you are going to be a problem out there?
It is probably a bit of both.
On one hand, yes, he is the king.
If the king dies, the war is over.
The rebellion wins instantly.
So he is the biggest target.
Right.
They have to protect him.
But on the other hand, think about who they are fighting.
They are fighting his kid.
Exactly.
The soldiers know David loves Absalom.
They might be very worried that David will hesitate.
If he sees his son on the battlefield, will he freeze?
Will he tell his men to stop fighting?
Right.
You cannot have a leader who is emotionally confused in the middle of a battle.
So they basically tell him to stay home.
They bench him.
You stay here.
We will do the fighting.
And David listens to them.
He agrees to stay behind.
The text says he stood by the gate.
And the gate is a very important symbol here.
Can we talk about that?
Because when we say gate today, we just mean a door and a fence.
Yes.
But in an ancient city, the gate was the center of everything.
It was where the judges sat.
It was the market.
It was where important decisions were made.
But here, the story uses the gate to show us how lonely David is.
It is a space in between.
He is not hiding deep inside the city.
But he is not out on the battlefield either.
He is just stuck on the edge.
He is a king, but he is not leading his men.
He is a father, but he is sending an army to destroy his son's army.
He is just watching.
As the army marches out past him, he stops the three commanders.
He gives them one order.
And he makes sure everyone hears him.
This order is so important.
He says,
Deal gently for my sake with the young man Absalom.
Deal gently.
Let's just think about those words.
Yeah.
These men have swords.
They are going out into a forest to fight to the death.
It is kill or be killed.
And the king says,
Be gentle.
It really clashes with the reality of war.
It makes no sense militarily.
If I am a regular soldier in that army and I hear the king say that,
I think I would be angry.
Why would you be angry?
Because Absalom started this war.
Absalom is trying to kill me and my friends.
He forced us to leave our homes.
And now the king is saying,
Please do not hurt the guy who is causing all this pain.
It feels like he cares more about his enemy's son than his loyal soldiers.
And that is the huge problem right there.
David is putting his role as a father above his role as a king.
To win this war and make the country safe,
they have to get rid of the rebel leader.
But David is saying,
Win the war, but do not hurt the leader.
It is an impossible puzzle.
And look closely at the words David uses.
He calls him the young man Absalom.
Yeah, he does not call him the rebel general.
Right.
This is something Robert Alter points out.
It is called characterization through dialogue.
What does that mean exactly?
It means we learn about a character's mind just by the words they choose to speak.
By calling him the young man, David is trying to erase what Absalom has done.
He refuses to see him as a dangerous enemy.
He is just picturing a confused teenager, not a grown man,
trying to kill him.
He is trying to steal a kingdom.
Exactly.
He is lying to himself.
But this puts his general, Joab, in a terrible position.
Joab is very practical.
Joab knows that if they let Absalom live, the war will never really end.
Because Absalom is not going to stop.
He is completely committed to taking power.
Right.
So if Joab lets him live, the country stays broken.
But if Joab kills him to fix the country, he is breaking a direct order from his king.
It is a trap for Joab.
No matter what he does, he is in trouble.
And verse 5,
it tells us that all the people heard the king give this order.
David did not say it in secret.
He announced it to everyone.
So now the whole army knows the king's weakness.
With that confusing order in their heads, they march into the woods of Ephraim.
And this is where the text gets really chaotic.
It says the battle was scattered over the face of all the country.
So this is not two neat lines of soldiers fighting on a flat field.
No, this is messy fighting in the bushes and the trees.
And the terrain, the forest itself, gives David's men a big advantage.
Why is that?
Because David and his generals, like Joab, spent years hiding in the wilderness when they were younger.
They know how to fight in rough places.
They are tough, experienced soldiers.
And what about Absalom's army?
Absalom's army is mostly regular people who joined the rebellion because Absalom was popular.
They are city people.
They might have more men, but they do not know how to fight in a dark, rocky forest.
So they go into these woods, and it is a complete disaster for them.
They are tripping over roots, falling into holes, getting separated.
And that is why the text says the forest devoured them.
It ate them up.
It is a way of saying the natural world itself fought against the rebellion.
That brings us to the most famous image in this whole chapter.
Maybe one of the most famous images in the Bible.
The mule, the big tree, and Absalom.
Yes.
Let's break down each part of this image because every detail matters in the story.
First, Absalom is riding a mule.
Now, when I picture a king or a prince in battle,
I picture a big, beautiful, and beautiful woman.
A beautiful horse.
Why a mule?
In that culture and time, the mule was the animal that the king rode.
It was a symbol of royal power.
Oh, really?
Yes.
So while riding a mule into battle, Absalom is sending a loud message.
He is saying, look at me.
I am the king now.
Okay, so it is a status symbol.
He is showing off.
Exactly.
And then he rides into a great terebinth tree.
This is a type of oak tree.
They are massive, and they have very thick, low branches that twist around.
Wow.
He rides under this huge tree, and the text says his head got caught in the branches.
Now, if you look at paintings or listen to popular stories about this, what usually gets caught?
His hair.
Everyone always says he got caught by his long hair.
Right.
Now, the actual Hebrew word here translates to head.
It says his head caught fast.
But the story strongly wants us to think about his hair.
Why is that?
Because earlier in the story of 2 Samuel, the narrator took the time to tell us all about Absalom's hair.
It was famously beautiful and heavy.
He only cut it once a year.
He didn't cut it twice a year, because it grew so much.
It was his best feature.
He was very proud of it.
Yes.
It was his vanity.
The thing he loved most about himself.
So in terms of telling a good story, this is a technique.
When a writer shows you something important early on, they usually bring it back at the
end.
So his pride, the thing that made him feel special, is the very thing that traps him.
That is the tragic irony.
And then the story gives us another powerful picture.
The mule keeps going.
The mule just walks right out from under him.
Think about what that means.
The mule is the symbol of the kingdom.
And the symbol of the kingdom just walks away, leaving him behind.
Wow.
That is such a strong visual.
He is left hanging there.
And the text says he was suspended between heaven and earth.
That phrase, between heaven and earth, is very deliberate.
He does not belong to the earth anymore.
He has no ground to stand on.
And he is not accepted by heaven either.
Because hanging on a tree was considered a curse, right?
Yes.
In their culture, being hung on a tree meant you were cursed.
So physically, he is stuck.
But spiritually and politically, he is also completely stuck and rejected by everything.
So he is just hanging there, alive, unable to get down.
And a regular soldier from David's army sees him.
Just a nameless man.
This guy looks up and sees the leader of the enemy army stuck in a tree.
This is the ultimate chance, right?
He could kill him, end the war, and be a huge hero.
But he does not touch him.
Instead, he runs to find David.
He runs to find Joab, the general.
He tells Joab, I just saw Absalom hanging in an oak tree.
And Joab is shocked.
Joab says, you saw him.
Why didn't you strike him to the ground right there?
I would have given you ten pieces of silver and a special belt.
Joab is thinking about the practical result.
He is thinking about a reward for solving the problem.
But the soldier gives a really amazing answer.
Yeah, the soldier basically says, you could give me a thousand pieces of silver and I
still would not touch him.
Because we all heard the king give his order, the king said to protect the young man.
This creates a fantastic contrast between two characters.
How so?
Well, look at what they are loyal to.
The common soldier is loyal to the king's personal word.
He cares more about what David said than he cares about money or being a hero.
He respects the father's wish.
Yes.
But Joab is different.
Joab is loyal to the king's throne.
He is loyal to the country.
He knows that David's wish to save his son is dangerous for the whole nation.
So, Joab has to choose between...
Obeying his friend and boss, or doing what is best for the country.
And Joab makes his choice very quickly.
He basically tells the soldier, I'm not going to waste time talking to you.
He grabs three darts, or small spears, and he goes straight to the tree.
And he thrusts them into Absalom's heart while he is still alive in the branches.
Three spears?
That seems like a lot.
It is very violent.
It is not just a battle anymore, it is an execution.
Joab is making sure the rebellion is completely dead.
And after that, Joab's armor-bearers finish him off.
And then we get the detail about how they buried him.
Right.
They do not take him back to the city.
They take his body, throw it into a big, deep pit right there in the dark forest.
And they throw a huge pile of rocks on top of him.
Why the rocks?
Is that just to cover him up quickly?
No.
A heap of stones was a sign of deep shame.
It was how they buried criminals.
It is the exact opposite of how a prince should be buried.
And the text points out something really sad here, right?
It mentions a monument.
Yes.
The narrator adds a little note.
It reminds us that when Absalom was alive, he built a beautiful stone pillar for himself
in a nice valley near the city.
Because he did not have a son to carry on his name, so he built a monument so people
would remember how great he was.
He wanted a marble statue in the sun.
He wanted glory.
But instead, he gets a pile of rough rocks in a dark ditch.
His pride led him to total shame.
So Absalom is dead.
The war is over.
Joab blows the trumpet to stop the fighting.
If this was a normal action movie, this is where the music would play and everyone would
go home happy.
The bad guy lost.
The good guys won.
But the chapter is only half finished.
That is right.
And this is where the style of the story completely changes.
It stops being the story about a war, and it turns into a psychological drama.
We stop looking at the battlefield, and we focus entirely on how the news is going to
get back to David.
The Race of the Runners.
When I first read this part, I was so confused.
Why does the Bible spend so many verses talking about two guys running?
It is a technique to build trust.
To build suspense.
Think about what we know as the readers.
We know Absalom is dead.
Joab knows.
The army knows.
But David does not know.
Exactly.
David, the main character, is still sitting at the gate waiting.
The writer wants to stretch out that waiting period as long as possible to make us feel
the tension.
Okay, so we have two runners who want to take the news to the king.
The first one is a young man named Ahimaaz.
Who is he?
Ahimaaz is the son of the high priest.
He is an insider.
He is well known.
He is respected.
And he comes to Joab and says, let me run and tell the king the good news.
He thinks it is good news.
Right.
He thinks we won.
The rebellion is over.
The king will be so happy and I want to be the one to tell him.
He does not understand the emotional reality of the situation.
He is a bit naive.
Very naive.
But Joab is not naive.
Joab looks at Ahimaaz and says, no, you are not going to be the messenger today.
Why does Joab stop him?
Because Joab knows David.
Joab knows that the political news is good, but the personal news is terrible.
He knows David is going to be completely crushed.
And Joab probably likes Ahimaaz.
He does not want this nice young kid to be the person who breaks the king's heart.
Exactly.
So Joab looks for someone else.
He calls for a man.
The text just calls the Cushite.
The Cushite.
What does that tell us?
Cush was a region south of Egypt, likely modern day Ethiopia or Sudan.
So this man is a foreigner.
He is an outsider.
He is not part of the special inner circle in Jerusalem.
So Joab thinks if the king gets mad and decides to punish the messenger, it is better.
If it is this outsider rather than the high priest's son.
It is a very cold political decision.
So the Cushite starts running, but Ahimaaz will not give up.
He begs Joab, please let me run too.
He is acting like a little kid who wants to join a game.
And finally, Joab gives up.
He says, fine, run.
And this is where Robert Alter's analysis is so wonderful.
He points out the specific details the narrator uses to slow down the story.
Right.
The details about the roads they take.
The text tells us that Ahimaaz ran by the way of the plane.
What does that mean for the race?
The plane is the flat valley floor.
It is a longer distance, but it is flat and easy to run on.
The Cushite, who left first, took the direct path, which goes over the rough hills.
So the Cushite is running up and down steep mountains while Ahimaaz is running on a smooth,
flat road.
Yes.
So even though he left later and took a longer route, Ahimaaz runs faster.
The text says he overran the Cushite.
He passes him.
And again, we spent all this time on the geography.
And the running styles.
Why?
Robert Alter calls this retardation.
It means slowing the action down on purpose.
We are waiting with David.
We are wondering who is going to get there first.
What are they going to say?
It really works.
It makes you feel anxious.
So the story shifts back to David.
He is sitting between the inner and outer gates of the city.
He is just waiting.
And there is a watchman up on the roof of the gate.
The watchman looks out and sees a man running alone.
He shouts down to the king.
And David says, if he is alone, he has news.
Meaning, if the army had lost, there would be hundreds of men running away together.
One man running alone means he is a messenger.
Then the watchman sees the second runner and he says, the running of the first man looks
like the running of Ahimaaz.
That is funny.
He can recognize him from far away just by how he moves.
Ahimaaz must have had a very distinct way of running.
But the important part is David's reaction to hearing that name.
David says he is a good man and he comes with good news.
This is a classic example of magical thinking.
Explain that.
What is magical thinking here?
David is so afraid of what the news might be that he is trying to control reality with
his mind.
He is telling himself, I like Ahimaaz.
Ahimaaz is a nice person.
Therefore the news he brings must be nice news.
He is connecting the personality of the messenger to the content of the message.
Exactly.
It makes no logical sense.
But it makes perfect emotional sense for a father who is terrified.
He is living in denial.
So Ahimaaz gets to the gate first.
He is totally out of breath.
He falls down on his face in front of the king and he shouts one single word, which
means peace or all is well.
And then he gives this big happy report.
He says, praise the Lord your God who has handed over the man who rebelled against the
king.
He did it.
He delivered the victory speech.
But look at David's response.
David does not say thank you.
He does not praise God.
He does not celebrate the victory.
He ignores all of that.
He only asks one question.
Is the young man Absalom safe?
He goes right back to that phrase, the young man.
And what does Ahimaaz do?
He totally freezes.
He chokes.
He says, well, I saw great commotion when Joab sent me, but I did not know what it was.
Is he lying to the king?
He is almost certainly lying or at least dodging the truth out of fear.
He saw Joab.
He knew the war was over.
He knew Absalom was stuck in the tree.
But when he actually looked into David's eyes, he lost all his courage.
The reality of the pain was too much for him.
Right.
He wanted the fun part of the job, the glory of bringing good news.
But he did not have the bravery to deliver the terrible truth.
He represents how hard it is for humans to give bad news to people we care about.
So David just tells him, step aside and stand over there.
And Ahimaaz has to stand there in silence.
He failed his mission.
And then the second runner arrives, the Cushite.
The Cushite comes in breathing heavy.
He says, good news for my lord, the king.
The Lord has rescued you today.
From all who rebelled against you.
He gives basically the same report as Ahimaaz.
And David asks him the exact same question, word for word.
Is the young man Absalom safe?
Hearing the text repeat that question is so painful.
That is another of Robert Alter's points about biblical narrative.
Repetition is never an accident.
The writer forces us to hear the question twice to build the tension to the absolute
maximum limit.
We feel the desperation.
And the Cushite has to answer.
Hmm.
But he does not just say, no, he is dead.
He gives a very careful answer.
It is a brilliant piece of speaking.
He says, may the enemies of my lord, the king and all who rise up to harm you be like that
young man.
It is poetic, but it is very clear.
Yes.
He uses a comparison.
He wishes all enemies would end up like Absalom.
He does not say the harsh words he is dead, but the meaning hits David instantly.
And by saying it this way, the Cushite is trying to remind David politely that this young
man was an enemy.
He is framing the death as a good thing, as justice for the king.
But David does not care about justice in that moment.
He does not hear the word enemy.
He only understands that his son is gone.
And this brings us to verse 33.
The final verse of the chapter.
It is the absolute climax of the story.
The reaction.
The text says the king was much moved.
The Hebrew word used there suggests a physical reaction.
He is shaking violently.
He is trembling.
He cannot even stay in the public space anymore.
He walks up the stairs to the little room over the gate.
And as he walks, he starts crying out.
And the words he cries are considered one of the most famous expressions of grief in
all of literature.
He cries, Oh, my son, Absalom, my son, my son, Absalom, if only I had died in your place.
Oh, Absalom, my son, my son.
Let's look at the structure of that cry.
Robert Alter points out how repetitive it is.
Count the words.
He says the word son five times.
Five times in one breath.
And he says the name Absalom three times.
It is like he is stuck in a loop.
He cannot move past those words.
The sentence structure is a circle.
He just goes round and round the name and his relationship to him.
There is no politics in this cry.
There is no relief that his kingdom is safe.
None at all.
It is just the raw, primal scream of a parent who has lost a child.
The story is fully transitioned into a tragedy.
And that middle phrase, if only I had died in your place, that really hits hard because
if we think about the story up to this point, David probably feels really responsible for
this, doesn't he?
He absolutely does.
This civil war did not happen by magic.
It happened because of years of family problems.
David failed to discipline his children in the past.
He failed to be a present father to Absalom.
So the rebellion is kind of his fault?
Yes.
The war is a giant consequence of his own mistakes.
So when he says, I wish I had died instead of you, he's not just being dramatic.
He deeply feels that he's the one who deserved the punishment.
He feels like the spears that hit Absalom were actually meant for him.
That is the core of the tragedy.
The kingdom is saved, but the king is completely destroyed.
It ruins the victory.
Think about the soldiers.
They just survived a horrible battle in a terrifying forest.
They fought hard and they won.
They expect to come home to a celebration.
They expect music and cheering.
But instead they walk up to the city and they hear the king crying upstairs.
It makes them feel like they did something wrong.
The story tells us the victory was turned into mourning that day.
The army sneaks into the city quietly.
Like people who are ashamed.
Like people who ran away from a fight.
That is so sad for the troops.
They risked their lives to save David, and David is crying for the person who tried to
murder them.
And this brings up a fascinating point about
God's role in this specific chapter.
Have you noticed anything missing?
Now that you mention it, God is very quiet.
Yes.
God is not a speaking character in chapter 18.
There are no prophets delivering messages.
There are no supernatural miracles.
No lightning from the sky.
Everything feels very normal in chapter 18.
It is just people running, fighting, and making choices.
Exactly.
But the narrator still wants us to feel God's presence in the background.
The way the forest fights against the rebels.
The way Absalom's own pride traps him in the tree.
The text presents these natural events as God's judgment working through normal life.
God is directing the outcome, but the humans are making all the mistakes themselves.
Yes.
The human errors are the tools used to finish the story.
I want to go back to the runners for a second.
Ahimaaz really sticks with me.
I feel like his failure to deliver the news teaches us something important.
I think so too.
Ahimaaz wanted to bring the good news, but he wanted to completely ignore the painful part.
He wanted the victory without the death.
But in this story, you cannot separate them.
The victory was the death.
That is a profound way to say it.
The victory was the death of the Son.
And many people who study the Bible see a deep connection here to the whole message of the Bible.
You mean connecting it fully?
Yes.
Think about the images.
You have a king crying over a son who dies on a tree.
The king says, I wish I could have died in your place.
But David was helpless.
He could not take his son's place.
Absalom had to die for his own crimes.
Right.
But in the New Testament story, you have a king who actually does take the place of the rebels.
A son who dies on a tree, not for his own rebellion, but to save the people who rebelled against him.
Oh, wow.
So David's cry is an unfulfilled one.
Exactly.
It is a wish he cannot make come true.
And the Christian gospel presents itself as the answer to that wish, the king who does die for the rebels.
That adds so much weight to this scene at the gate.
It is the grief of total helplessness.
The grief of winning a war but losing what matters most.
In a civil war, especially a family war, even when you win, you lose.
So we have covered a massive amount of ground today, strictly staying in Chapter 18.
We looked at the terrifying forest, the confusing order from the king.
The royal mule, the tree, the race, and that heartbreaking cry.
It is an incredibly rich piece of writing.
What is your biggest takeaway for the listener today, if they only remember one thing from our deep dive into 2 Samuel 18?
For me, it is the absolute brilliance of the narrative art.
The way the story uses delay to build tension with the runners, and the way it uses repetition.
Right, forcing us to hear the question twice.
Yes.
The writer does not give us a long explanation of David's feelings.
He just lets David repeat the word son five times.
It shows us the pain instead of just telling us about it.
It trusts the reader to feel the weight of those words.
I love that.
And for me, my big takeaway is that image of David stuck at the gate.
He is caught right in the middle.
He is stuck between being the boss of a country and being a dad to his kid.
It is a terrible place to be.
I think anyone who leads people or anyone who is a parent can relate to that feeling a little bit.
Sometimes you have a job you have to do and you have to be strict.
But you are also a human being with a heart that breaks for the people you are leading.
And that links to a really provocative thought for everyone listening to Chew on.
Can you ever truly separate the job you do from the person you are?
Joab thought you could.
Joab shut off all his feelings to save the country.
But David could not shut off his feelings.
He let his heart rule his actions.
So who was right?
The tough general who saved the nation or the father who could not stop loving his broken son?
The story really does not give us an easy answer, does it?
No, it leaves us with a tension.
The world needs people like Joab to keep order.
But a world without the love of people like David is not a world you want to live in.
That is a heavy thought to end on.
Well, we are going to leave you there, sitting at the gate with David.
I really encourage you to open your Bible this week and read 2 Samuel chapter 18 for yourself.
But take your time with it.
Do not just speed read it.
Exactly.
Watch how the forest moves.
